name: Lebanon
tagline: From the Phoenician harbours of Tyre, Sidon and Byblos to the confessional republic of the cedars.
summary: Lebanon is a narrow, mountainous land on the eastern Mediterranean whose coastal cities—Byblos, Sidon and Tyre—were the great harbours of Phoenicia, seafaring traders who carried the alphabet across the ancient world. Its rugged interior long sheltered marginalised communities, above all the Maronite Christians and the Druze, and under the Maan and Shihab emirs Mount Lebanon developed a distinct autonomy within the Ottoman Empire. After the 1860 sectarian conflict and the collapse of the Ottoman order, France created the State of Greater Lebanon in 1920, and the country won full independence on 22 November 1943 under an unwritten National Pact that distributed power among its religious communities. Prosperity made mid-century Beirut a regional financial and cultural capital, but a devastating civil war (1975–1990), foreign interventions, and a catastrophic economic collapse and port explosion after 2019 have severely tested the republic into the present.

founding.label: Independence from France
founding.detail: Modern Lebanon dates its sovereignty to the autumn of 1943. After elections, the new government of President Bechara El Khoury and Prime Minister Riad al-Solh amended the constitution to remove the articles enshrining the French Mandate. The French authorities responded on 11 November 1943 by arresting the president, prime minister and several ministers, but mass protests and British and international pressure forced their release on 22 November 1943—now celebrated as Independence Day. Power was shared under the National Pact, an unwritten understanding reached the same year by El Khoury, a Maronite, and al-Solh, a Sunni, which balanced the country's confessional communities. The United States formally recognised Lebanese independence on 8 September 1944, and the last French troops withdrew at the end of 1946.

quickFacts.label: Phoenician cities
quickFacts.value: Byblos, Sidon and Tyre — UNESCO World Heritage Sites

quickFacts.label: Political system
quickFacts.value: Confessional parliamentary republic (National Pact, 1943; reformed by the Taif Agreement, 1989)

quickFacts.label: Roman heritage
quickFacts.value: Baalbek (Heliopolis), among the grandest Roman sanctuaries — UNESCO World Heritage Site

quickFacts.label: Religious communities
quickFacts.value: Maronite and other Christians, Sunni and Shi'a Muslims, and Druze

eras.title: Phoenicia: Byblos, Sidon and Tyre
eras.standfirst: The Canaanite harbour cities of the Lebanese coast became the seafaring Phoenicians, who spread the alphabet, Tyrian purple and Mediterranean trade.
body: The coast of present-day Lebanon held some of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world. By the third millennium BCE Byblos was a flourishing port trading cedar timber with Egypt, and along the shore rose the other great Canaanite centres—Berytus (Beirut), Sidon and Tyre. The Greeks would later call these mariners Phoenicians, after the purple dye, Tyrian purple, extracted from murex shells that became one of their most coveted exports. Each city was an independent state with its own king and patron deity: Baal and Baalat at Byblos, Eshmun at Sidon, and Melqart at Tyre.

The Phoenicians' most enduring legacy was the alphabet. At Byblos a linear alphabetic script of twenty-two consonantal signs was in use by the early first millennium BCE, and almost all of the earliest known Phoenician inscriptions come from the site. Adopted and adapted by the Greeks, this writing system became the ancestor of the Greek and Latin alphabets and, through them, of much of the world's scripts. From their narrow coastal base the Phoenicians became the Mediterranean's foremost merchants, sailors and colonisers.

From the ninth century BCE Tyre and Sidon planted trading colonies across the Mediterranean, the greatest of them Carthage in North Africa, traditionally founded in 814 BCE. The home cities prospered as crossroads between the great empires, paying tribute in turn to Assyria, Babylon and Persia while retaining their commercial vigour. Tyre famously withstood a long siege by Nebuchadnezzar II before its independence finally ended; in 332 BCE Alexander the Great captured the island city after building a causeway to its walls, bringing the age of the autonomous Phoenician states to a close.

events.title: Byblos trades cedar with Egypt
events.summary: Byblos flourishes as a port exporting Lebanese cedar to Egypt, beginning a record of urban habitation that makes it one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world.

events.title: The Phoenician alphabet at Byblos
events.summary: A twenty-two letter alphabetic script comes into use at Byblos; adopted by the Greeks, it becomes the ancestor of the Greek and Latin alphabets and the writing of much of the world.

events.title: Tyre founds Carthage
events.summary: Tyrian colonists are traditionally credited with founding Carthage, the most powerful of the Phoenician colonies in the western Mediterranean.

events.title: Alexander the Great takes Tyre
events.summary: After a long siege and the building of a causeway to the island city, Alexander captures Tyre, ending the independence of the Phoenician city-states.

pullquote.text: Byblos is directly associated with the history and diffusion of the Phoenician alphabet.
pullquote.attribution: UNESCO World Heritage Centre on Byblos

eras.title: Roman Heliopolis and the Rise of Christianity
eras.standfirst: Under Greek and then Roman rule the coast prospered, Baalbek became a colossal sanctuary, and Christianity took root—giving rise to the Maronite community of Mount Lebanon.
body: After Alexander, the Lebanese coast passed to his Hellenistic successors and, from the first century BCE, to Rome. The region grew prosperous within the empire: Berytus became a Roman colony famous for its school of law, while the city of Baalbek, refounded as the colony of Heliopolis, was endowed over more than two centuries with one of the largest temple complexes of the Roman world. Pilgrims thronged to its sanctuaries of Jupiter, Bacchus and Venus, the so-called Heliopolitan Triad; the Temple of Bacchus survives as one of the best-preserved Roman temples anywhere.

Christianity spread early along this coast, where the cities of Tyre and Sidon are named in the Gospels. From the new faith emerged a community that would shape Lebanon for centuries: the Maronites, who traced their origins to Saint Maron, a Syrian ascetic of the late fourth and early fifth centuries, and to the monastery founded in his name on the Orontes. Drawn into the doctrinal disputes that divided eastern Christianity, the Maronites gradually migrated from Syria into the rugged northern mountains of Lebanon, whose isolation offered refuge.

Mount Lebanon's broken terrain became a sanctuary for communities marginalised elsewhere, a pattern that defined the country's distinctive religious mosaic. The Maronites established a self-governing church under their own patriarch and clung to their mountain strongholds through every subsequent change of empire. When Arab Muslim armies swept through the Levant in the 630s, defeating Byzantium, the highlands retained a Christian character even as the wider region was transformed, setting the stage for the long coexistence of mountain peoples that lay ahead.

events.title: Baalbek becomes Roman Heliopolis
events.summary: Refounded as a Roman colony, Baalbek is endowed over more than two centuries with colossal temples to Jupiter, Bacchus and Venus, becoming one of the most famous sanctuaries of the Roman world.

events.title: Saint Maron and the Maronite origins
events.summary: The Maronite community traces its origin to Saint Maron, a Syrian hermit whose ascetic following grows into a distinct church that later takes refuge in the mountains of Lebanon.

events.title: Arab Muslim conquest of the Levant
events.summary: Arab armies defeat Byzantium and bring the Levant under Muslim rule, while the Christian communities of Mount Lebanon retain their distinct character in the highlands.

figures.name: Saint Maron
figures.blurb: A Syrian hermit of the late fourth and early fifth centuries whose ascetic disciples grew into the Maronite community, which later established itself in the mountains of Lebanon.

pullquote.text: Its colossal constructions built over a period of more than two centuries make it one of the most famous sanctuaries of the Roman world.
pullquote.attribution: UNESCO World Heritage Centre on Baalbek

eras.title: The Emirate of Mount Lebanon
eras.standfirst: Under Ottoman suzerainty the Maan and Shihab emirs forged a semi-autonomous Mount Lebanon; Fakhr al-Din II, often called the father of modern Lebanon, first united its Druze and Maronite districts.
body: When the Ottomans conquered Syria in 1516 they left the mountain largely to local chieftains, and from this looser order rose the emirate of Mount Lebanon. Its most remarkable ruler was Fakhr al-Din II of the Druze Maan dynasty (c. 1572–1635), who for the first time brought the Druze and Maronite districts of the mountains under a single authority. Although a Druze, he won the support of the Maronites and by 1631 dominated much of Syria, Lebanon and Palestine. He encouraged trade and silk production, cultivated ties with the Medici grand dukes of Tuscany, and is frequently regarded as the father of modern Lebanon.

Fakhr al-Din's growing power alarmed the Ottomans, who sent an army against him; he was defeated in 1633, captured the following year and executed in Constantinople in 1635. Yet the union of the Druze and Maronite districts that he had forged survived his fall. When the Maan line died out at the end of the seventeenth century, leadership passed to the related Shihab family, who continued to govern the mountain as paramount emirs and tax-farmers under Ottoman overlordship into the nineteenth century.

The most powerful of the Shihabs was Bashir Shihab II (1767–1850), who ruled from Beiteddine for roughly half a century from 1788. Born into a branch of the family that had embraced Maronite Christianity, he was the emirate's only Maronite ruler, presiding over a shift from feudal tenures toward a cash-crop and handicraft economy centred on silk. For a time he allied with the Egyptian invader Ibrahim Pasha, but when Egyptian rule in Syria collapsed under European and Ottoman pressure in 1840, Bashir II was deposed and exiled, and the emirate that had given Mount Lebanon its autonomy came to an end.

events.title: Ottoman conquest of Syria
events.summary: The Ottomans defeat the Mamluks and take Syria and Lebanon, but leave Mount Lebanon largely to local emirs, allowing a semi-autonomous emirate to develop.

events.title: Fakhr al-Din II unites the mountain
events.summary: Fakhr al-Din II of the Druze Maan dynasty unites the Druze and Maronite districts of Mount Lebanon, expands his domains across the Levant and cultivates ties with Tuscany.

events.title: Execution of Fakhr al-Din II
events.summary: Defeated by Ottoman forces wary of his power, Fakhr al-Din is captured and executed in Constantinople, but the union of Druze and Maronite districts he created endures.

events.title: Fall of Bashir Shihab II
events.summary: After siding with Egypt's Ibrahim Pasha, the long-ruling emir Bashir Shihab II is deposed and exiled when Egyptian rule in Syria collapses, ending the autonomous emirate.

figures.name: Fakhr al-Din II
figures.blurb: Druze prince who first united the Druze and Maronite districts of Mount Lebanon and is often called the father of modern Lebanon; executed by the Ottomans in 1635.

figures.name: Bashir Shihab II
figures.blurb: The only Maronite ruler of the emirate, he governed Mount Lebanon from Beiteddine for some fifty years before being deposed and exiled in 1840.

pullquote.text: For the first time he united the Druze and Maronite districts of the Lebanon Mountains under his personal rule.
pullquote.attribution: On Fakhr al-Din II, the father of modern Lebanon

eras.title: 1860, the Mutasarrifate and the French Mandate
eras.standfirst: Sectarian violence in 1860 brought European intervention and an autonomous province; after the Ottoman collapse France created Greater Lebanon in 1920, leading to independence in 1943.
body: The fall of the emirate left Mount Lebanon divided and unsettled. Tensions between Druze landholders and Maronite peasants—sharpened by social change and rival European patronage—erupted in 1860 into a brutal sectarian conflict in which thousands of Christians were killed in the mountain and in Damascus. The figures are disputed, but contemporaries estimated many thousands of deaths. The bloodshed prompted a French-led international intervention, and under European pressure the Ottomans established in 1861 an autonomous Mount Lebanon, the Mutasarrifate, governed by a Christian official and a council representing the sects.

This special status lasted until the First World War, when famine devastated the mountain and the Ottomans imposed direct rule. After the empire's defeat, the victorious powers placed Syria and Lebanon under a French mandate confirmed by the League of Nations. On 1 September 1920 the French high commissioner General Henri Gouraud proclaimed the State of Greater Lebanon, enlarging the old autonomous province with Beirut, Tripoli, Sidon, Tyre and the Bekaa valley. The new borders folded large Sunni, Shi'a and Greek Orthodox populations into a state where Maronites remained the largest single community.

In 1926 Greater Lebanon was reorganised as the Lebanese Republic with a constitution, though France retained ultimate authority. Nationalist pressure mounted, and during the Second World War the Free French promised independence. Elections in 1943 brought to power a government determined to end the mandate; when France arrested the new leaders that November, popular protest and international pressure forced their release, and Lebanon emerged as a sovereign state governed by the confessional National Pact.

events.title: Sectarian conflict in Mount Lebanon
events.summary: Violence between Druze and Maronites spreads to Damascus and kills many thousands of Christians (figures are disputed estimates), prompting a French-led European intervention.

events.title: The Mount Lebanon Mutasarrifate
events.summary: Under European pressure the Ottomans grant Mount Lebanon autonomy as the Mutasarrifate, governed by a Christian official and a council of the religious communities.

events.title: Creation of Greater Lebanon
events.summary: French High Commissioner Henri Gouraud proclaims the State of Greater Lebanon, enlarging the old province with the coastal cities and the Bekaa under a French mandate.

events.title: The Lebanese Republic and constitution
events.summary: Greater Lebanon is reorganised as the Lebanese Republic with a written constitution, though France retains ultimate authority under the mandate.

figures.name: Henri Gouraud
figures.role: French High Commissioner for Syria and Lebanon
figures.blurb: French general who proclaimed the State of Greater Lebanon on 1 September 1920, defining the borders of the modern country under the French mandate.

eras.title: Independence and the Golden Age
eras.standfirst: An independent Lebanon governed by the National Pact prospered as the financial and cultural hub of the region, with Beirut hailed as the Paris of the Middle East.
body: Independent Lebanon was built on a delicate confessional compromise. The unwritten National Pact of 1943, agreed between the Maronite president Bechara El Khoury and the Sunni prime minister Riad al-Solh, reserved the presidency for a Maronite, the premiership for a Sunni and the speakership of parliament for a Shi'a, and balanced the country's Western and Arab orientations. The United States recognised Lebanon's independence in September 1944, and the last French troops left at the end of 1946, completing the transition to sovereignty.

Freed from the mandate, Lebanon flourished. Liberal banking laws, a free-market economy and a strategic position between East and West made Beirut the financial centre of the Middle East and a magnet for trade, tourism and the press. From the mid-1950s into the early 1970s—remembered as Lebanon's golden age—the capital was celebrated as the Paris of the Middle East, a cosmopolitan city of universities, publishers, beaches and nightlife whose glamour drew visitors from across the region and beyond.

Yet beneath the prosperity strains accumulated. The 1932 census on which confessional shares were based was never repeated, even as the demographic balance shifted; wealth was unevenly distributed; and the country was buffeted by regional currents, including the influx of Palestinian refugees and the growing presence of Palestinian guerrilla organisations after 1967. Sharp disputes over the role of the armed Palestinian factions, and over the confessional system itself, steadily polarised Lebanese politics and brought the republic to the brink of breakdown.

events.title: The National Pact
events.summary: Bechara El Khoury and Riad al-Solh agree the unwritten National Pact distributing the presidency, premiership and parliamentary speakership among Maronites, Sunnis and Shi'a.

events.title: U.S. recognition of Lebanon
events.summary: The United States formally recognises Lebanon as an independent state, and establishes diplomatic relations later that year.

events.title: French withdrawal completed
events.summary: The last French troops leave Lebanon, completing the country's transition to full sovereignty.

events.title: Beirut, the Paris of the Middle East
events.summary: Lebanon's golden age makes Beirut the banking, commercial and cultural capital of the region, renowned for its cosmopolitan prosperity.

figures.name: Bechara El Khoury
figures.blurb: Maronite statesman and the republic's first president, who with Riad al-Solh forged the 1943 National Pact and led Lebanon to independence from France.

figures.name: Riad al-Solh
figures.role: First prime minister of independent Lebanon
figures.blurb: Sunni nationalist and the republic's first prime minister, co-author of the National Pact, who anchored Lebanon's independence in an Arab orientation; assassinated in 1951.

pullquote.text: The National Pact was, in essence, a political compromise between the two major religious communities to obtain independence.
pullquote.attribution: Library of Congress, Lebanon: A Country Study

eras.title: Civil War, Foreign Intervention and Collapse
eras.standfirst: A fifteen-year civil war and successive interventions devastated the country; the Cedar Revolution, the rise of Hezbollah, and an economic collapse and port explosion have defined a fraught present.
body: Lebanon's civil war began on 13 April 1975 and lasted until 1990, drawing in a shifting array of confessional militias and outside powers. Estimates of the dead vary widely and are necessarily approximate, commonly placed in the range of roughly 120,000 to 150,000. Syrian forces entered in 1976, and Israel invaded in 1982, besieging Beirut and driving out the Palestine Liberation Organization; the assassination of president-elect Bashir Gemayel and the Sabra and Shatila massacre followed. The Taif Agreement of 1989 ended most of the fighting and adjusted the confessional balance, shifting power from the Maronite president toward the Sunni prime minister and cabinet.

The post-war Second Republic was rebuilt under Syrian tutelage. Prime Minister Rafic Hariri led an ambitious reconstruction of Beirut, but the country remained under Syrian military presence and the growing influence of Hezbollah, the Shi'a movement that had emerged during the war and the Israeli occupation of the south. Hariri's assassination by a car bomb on 14 February 2005, which killed him and many others, triggered the mass protests of the Cedar Revolution and led Syria to withdraw its troops after nearly three decades. A United Nations–backed tribunal later convicted a Hezbollah operative in absentia for the killing.

The years that followed brought recurring political deadlock, a 2006 war between Hezbollah and Israel, and spillover from the Syrian conflict. From 2019 Lebanon fell into one of the most severe economic crises in modern history, its currency and banking system collapsing; on 4 August 2020 a vast store of ammonium nitrate detonated at the port of Beirut, killing more than two hundred people and devastating the capital. After years of paralysis, parliament elected army commander Joseph Aoun president in January 2025, and a technocratic government under Prime Minister Nawaf Salam took up the tasks of reconstruction and reform amid renewed Israel–Hezbollah fighting and a November 2024 ceasefire, leaving Lebanon's recovery still uncertain into 2026.

events.title: The Lebanese Civil War
events.summary: Fighting erupts in Beirut and escalates into a fifteen-year civil war that draws in Syria, Israel and many militias; deaths are estimated, very approximately, in the range of 120,000–150,000.

events.title: Israeli invasion and siege of Beirut
events.summary: Israel invades Lebanon and besieges Beirut, forcing out the PLO; the assassination of president-elect Bashir Gemayel is followed by the Sabra and Shatila massacre.

events.title: The Taif Agreement
events.summary: Brokered with Arab support, the Taif Agreement winds down the war and rebalances the confessional system, shifting authority from the Maronite president to the Sunni prime minister and cabinet.

events.title: Hariri assassination and the Cedar Revolution
events.summary: A car bomb kills former prime minister Rafic Hariri and others; mass protests known as the Cedar Revolution lead Syria to withdraw its forces after nearly thirty years.

events.title: Economic collapse and the Beirut port explosion
events.summary: From 2019 Lebanon suffers one of the world's worst economic crises; on 4 August 2020 an ammonium-nitrate explosion at the port of Beirut kills more than 200 people and devastates the capital.

figures.name: Rafic Hariri
figures.blurb: Businessman and statesman who led the post-war reconstruction of Beirut as prime minister; his 2005 assassination triggered the Cedar Revolution and the Syrian withdrawal.

pullquote.text: His death... incited widespread protests known as the Cedar Revolution, which ultimately led to the withdrawal of Syrian troops after nearly 30 years.
pullquote.attribution: On the assassination of Rafic Hariri, 2005

figures.name: Fakhr al-Din II
figures.blurb: Druze prince who first united the Druze and Maronite districts of Mount Lebanon and extended his rule across the Levant before his execution by the Ottomans.

figures.name: Bechara El Khoury
figures.blurb: Maronite leader who, with Riad al-Solh, forged the 1943 National Pact and led Lebanon to independence from France.

figures.name: Rafic Hariri
figures.blurb: Architect of post-war reconstruction whose assassination sparked the Cedar Revolution and the end of Syrian military presence.
